text:
Refactoring is the process of improving existing computer code without changing what it does. The goal is to make the code cleaner and better organized. For example, you might rename unclear variable names or break a large function into smaller, simpler ones. The program’s behavior stays exactly the same.

This practice helps reduce “technical debt,” which builds up when you take shortcuts and write messy code. Over time, this debt makes it harder to add new features or fix bugs. By refactoring regularly, you improve readability, making the code easier for yourself and others to understand and maintain. It’s like tidying up a room so you can work more efficiently.
